section.title: Various Laws
section.sectionRationale: This collection of everyday case laws, lost property, mixed kinds, tassels, a bride's honor, sexual sin, protects the ordinary fabric of Israelite community life, showing that the LORD's covenant reaches into neighborliness, marriage, and even how a field is planted.
section.thesis: Because the LORD's covenant claims the whole of ordinary life, this chapter requires active neighborliness toward lost property, visible respect for the distinct kinds God has made, serious protection of a bride's honor, and sober justice for sexual sin, distinguishing consent from assault with real, if historically bounded, care for the vulnerable.
section.frameNote: Six movements: caring for a neighbor's lost animal or property; boundaries between men, women, and creatures reflecting creation's order; the ban on mixing seed, animals, and fabric, with a command to wear tassels; the defense of a bride's honor against false accusation; laws distinguishing adultery from assault; and a final forbidden marriage.

verses.text: If you see your brother's ox or sheep straying, you must not ignore it; be sure to return it to your brother.
If your brother does not live near you, or if you do not know who he is, you are to take the animal home to remain with you until your brother comes seeking it; then you can return it to him.
And you shall do the same for his donkey, his cloak, or anything your brother has lost and you have found. You must not ignore it.
If you see your brother's donkey or ox fallen on the road, you must not ignore it; you must help him lift it up.

units.label: Boundaries between men, women, and creatures

verses.text: A woman must not wear men's clothing, and a man must not wear women's clothing, for whoever does these things is detestable to the LORD your God.

If you come across a bird's nest with chicks or eggs, either in a tree or on the ground along the road, and the mother is sitting on the chicks or eggs, you must not take the mother along with the young.
You may take the young, but be sure to let the mother go, so that it may be well with you and that you may prolong your days.
If you build a new house, you are to construct a railing around your roof, so that you do not bring bloodguilt on your house if someone falls from it.

units.label: No mixing what God has distinguished
units.note: seed, animals, fabric, and the tassel of remembrance

verses.text: Do not plant your vineyard with two types of seed; if you do, the entire harvest will be defiled, both the crop you plant and the fruit of your vineyard.
Do not plow with an ox and a donkey yoked together.
Do not wear {{kelayim|clothes of wool and linen woven together}}.
You are to make {{tsitsit|tassels}} on the four corners of the cloak you wear.

units.label: A bride's honor defended
units.note: punishing false accusation, and true unfaithfulness

verses.text: Suppose a man marries a woman, has relations with her, and comes to hate her,
and he then accuses her of shameful conduct and gives her a bad name, saying, "I married this woman and had relations with her, but I discovered she was not a virgin."
Then the young woman's father and mother shall bring the proof of her {{betulim|virginity}} to the city elders at the gate
and say to the elders, "I gave my daughter in marriage to this man, but he has come to hate her.

And now he has accused her of shameful conduct, saying, 'I discovered that your daughter was not a virgin.' But here is the proof of her virginity." And they shall spread out the cloth before the city elders.
Then the elders of that city shall take the man and punish him.

They are also to fine him a hundred shekels of silver and give them to the young woman's father, because this man has given a virgin of Israel a bad name. And she shall remain his wife; he must not divorce her as long as he lives.
If, however, this accusation is true, and no proof of the young woman's virginity can be found,

she shall be brought to the door of her father's house, and there the men of her city will stone her to death. For she has committed an outrage in Israel by being promiscuous in her father's house. So you must purge the evil from among you.
If a man is found lying with another man's wife, both the man who slept with her and the woman must die. You must purge the evil from Israel.
If there is a virgin pledged in marriage to a man, and another man encounters her in the city and sleeps with her,

you must take both of them out to the gate of that city and stone them to death, the young woman because she did not cry out in the city, and the man because he has violated his neighbor's wife. So you must purge the evil from among you.
But if the man encounters a betrothed woman in the open country, and he overpowers her and lies with her, only the man who has done this must die.
Do nothing to the young woman, because she has committed no sin worthy of death. This case is just like one in which a man attacks his neighbor and murders him.
When he found her in the field, the betrothed woman cried out, but there was no one to save her.
If a man encounters a virgin who is not pledged in marriage, and he seizes her and lies with her, and they are discovered,
then the man who lay with her must pay the young woman's father fifty shekels of silver, and she must become his wife because he has violated her. He must not divorce her as long as he lives.
A man is not to marry his father's wife, so that he will not dishonor his father's marriage bed.

kelayim.gloss: Mixture, the mixing of distinct kinds. Applied to seeds, plow animals, and woven fabric alike, this pictures a created order of distinct kinds that Israel's daily life was to visibly respect, compare Leviticus 19:19.

tsitsit.gloss: Tassels, fringes worn on the four corners of a garment as a continual, visible reminder to keep all of the LORD's commandments, Numbers 15:38-39. A woman with a twelve-year hemorrhage later reaches out in faith to touch this very fringe on Jesus' cloak, Matthew 9:20-22.

betulim.gloss: The physical evidence, or state, of virginity. Used here in a legal dispute over a bride's honor, showing the seriousness with which Israel's law treated a false accusation against a woman's chastity.

basic.context: This chapter moves through a series of everyday laws: returning a neighbor's lost animal, keeping distinct boundaries between men and women and between kinds of creatures, wearing tassels as a reminder of the commandments, and then a cluster of laws about sexual purity, defending a falsely accused bride, punishing adultery, and distinguishing consensual sin from assault.
basic.hermeneutics: These are specific, situational case laws for ancient Israel, and some require careful, honest handling, especially the sexual assault laws in verses 23-27, which assume a woman's innocence when she could not have been heard crying for help, and the man's guilt regardless of location. The law's underlying concern is protecting the vulnerable and punishing the guilty, even though its ancient courtroom procedures, judging by whether a city could hear a cry, differ from how evidence is gathered today.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel is repeatedly told not to mix what God has distinguished, seed, plow animals, and woven fabric, kelayim, and instead to wear tsitsit, tassels, as a visible reminder of the commandments. A bride's honor is defended through the evidence of her betulim, virginity, when falsely questioned.
basic.typology: The tassels worn on the corners of the garment, meant to prompt obedience by sight, anticipate God's law written on the heart under the new covenant, Jeremiah 31:33, and directly connect to the woman who touched the very fringe of Jesus' garment and was healed, Matthew 9:20-22.
basic.christ: Matthew 9:20-22 records a woman reaching out to touch the tassel, the same tsitsit commanded in Deuteronomy 22:12, on Jesus' cloak, and being healed by faith. The garment that once reminded Israel to keep the law becomes, on Christ, the point of contact for a faith that receives what the law could never give.
basic.god: God's concern reaches into the smallest details of neighborliness and creation's order, and into the gravest matters of sexual sin. He is a God who defends the falsely accused, distinguishes real guilt from mere circumstance, and calls His people to active care rather than indifference toward a neighbor's loss.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 9:20-22 shows a woman touching the tassel of Jesus' garment in faith. Numbers 15:38-40 explains the purpose of tassels as a reminder to keep all the LORD's commandments. Exodus 22:1-15 gives parallel case law about lost or damaged property. Leviticus 19:19 states the same principle against mixing kinds.
basic.oneStory: God's covenant claims every corner of ordinary life, neighborliness, clothing, marriage, and justice for the vulnerable, and the small reminder of the tassel becomes, in Christ, the very place where faith reaches out and finds healing.

context.literary: This chapter continues the section of specific case laws following the appointment of Israel's leaders, moving through property, dress, and sexual ethics before the larger unit on community and worship in chapters 23-26.
context.historical: Ancient Israel's agrarian, tightly-knit community depended on this kind of practical law, returning lost animals, respecting boundaries, defending a bride's reputation, and punishing sexual sin, to hold together a functioning, just society.
context.canonical: The tassels commanded here become a recognizable feature of later Jewish life and, remarkably, the very point of contact for a woman's faith in the Gospels, when she reaches for the fringe of Jesus' garment.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To regulate ordinary Israelite life, from neighborliness and dress to marriage and sexual ethics, in ways that reflect the LORD's created order and protect the vulnerable, including a falsely accused bride and an assaulted woman.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: These are specific old covenant civil and ceremonial case laws describing Israel's particular legal setting, not a direct legal code for today; the underlying principles, active neighborliness, respect for creation's given order, protecting the vulnerable, and punishing real sexual sin, carry forward as enduring moral concerns even where the specific ancient procedures do not.

debates.question: How should the sexual assault laws in verses 23-27 be understood?

views.label: As protective case law reasoning from available evidence
views.case: The distinction between the city, where a cry could have been heard, and the open country, where it could not, reflects the ancient court's best available way of judging consent versus assault; in both settings the law assumes the woman's innocence where she could not have been rescued, holding the man solely guilty in the country case.

views.label: As a text requiring humble, careful handling today
views.case: Modern readers rightly recognize that the law's reasoning by circumstantial evidence is limited compared to a modern understanding of trauma and coercion, and should not use this text to minimize the reality or seriousness of assault regardless of location or a victim's ability to cry out.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the law's intent was to protect a genuinely assaulted woman and punish the guilty man, while acknowledging its ancient procedural markers do not map directly onto how the church or courts should evaluate cases of assault today.

study.originalLanguages: Israel is told not to mix what God has distinguished, kelayim, seed, plow animals, and woven fabric, verses 9-11, and instead to wear tsitsit, tassels, verse 12, as a visible reminder of the commandments. A bride's honor is defended through the evidence of her betulim, virginity, verse 15, when falsely questioned.

typology.type: The tassels on the corners of the garment
typology.fulfillment: The woman with the flow of blood reaches out in faith to touch this very fringe on Jesus' cloak and is healed, Matthew 9:20-22.

typology.type: Active care for a neighbor's lost property
typology.fulfillment: Christ's greater care as the good shepherd who goes after even one lost sheep, Luke 15:4-7.

study.christ: Matthew 9:20-22 records a woman reaching out to touch the tassel, the very tsitsit commanded in Deuteronomy 22:12, on Jesus' cloak, and being healed by faith. The garment that once reminded Israel to keep the law becomes, on Christ, the point of contact for a faith that receives what the law could never give.
study.god: God's concern reaches into the smallest details of neighborliness and creation's order, and into the gravest matters of sexual sin. He is a God who defends the falsely accused, distinguishes real guilt from mere circumstance, and calls His people to active care rather than indifference toward a neighbor's loss.

crossReferences.note: A woman touches the tassel of Jesus' garment in faith and is healed.
Explains the purpose of tassels as a visible reminder to keep all the LORD's commandments.
Gives parallel case law about lost or damaged property.
States the same principle against mixing distinct kinds.

study.oneStory: God's covenant claims every corner of ordinary life, neighborliness, clothing, marriage, and justice for the vulnerable, and the small reminder of the tassel becomes, in Christ, the very place where faith reaches out and finds healing.

quiz.q: What does the law require if you find your neighbor's lost ox or sheep straying?

options.text: You must not ignore it; you must return it to your neighbor or keep it safely until he comes looking for it.
options.note: Verses 1-3 give this explicit command.

options.text: You may keep it as your own after thirty days.
options.note: The text requires active return or safekeeping, not a waiting period leading to ownership.

options.text: You should report it to the elders and take no further action.
options.note: The text requires you personally take responsibility to return or hold it, not merely report it.

options.text: You have no responsibility toward it.
options.note: Verses 1-4 explicitly forbid ignoring it.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of wearing tassels on the corners of a garment, as explained in Numbers 15 and commanded in verse 12?

options.text: To serve as a visible, continual reminder to keep all the LORD's commandments.
options.note: Numbers 15:38-40 explains this purpose explicitly; Deuteronomy 22:12 gives the command itself.

options.text: To display wealth and social status.
options.note: The tassel's stated purpose is remembrance of the commandments, not status.

options.text: To identify a person's tribe only.
options.note: Its stated purpose is remembrance of obedience, not tribal identification.

options.text: No purpose is given anywhere in Scripture.
options.note: Numbers 15:38-40 gives an explicit purpose.

quiz.q: What happens if a husband falsely accuses his new wife of not being a virgin at marriage?

options.text: He is punished, fined a hundred shekels of silver paid to her father, and he may never divorce her.
options.note: Verses 18-19 state this explicit penalty.

options.text: The wife is punished instead of the husband.
options.note: The text explicitly punishes the husband for a false accusation, verses 18-19.

options.text: Nothing happens since it is his word against hers.
options.note: The text requires evidence and imposes real punishment for a false accusation.

options.text: The marriage is immediately dissolved with no penalty.
options.note: The text explicitly forbids him from ever divorcing her, verse 19.

quiz.q: How does the law distinguish a woman's guilt in cases of sexual assault based on location, verses 23-27?

options.text: In the city, where a cry could have been heard, both are judged guilty if she did not cry out; in the open country, where no one could hear her, only the man is guilty, and she is treated as blameless.
options.note: Verses 23-27 state this explicit distinction based on whether help could plausibly have been heard.

options.text: The location makes no difference; the woman is always held guilty.
options.note: The text explicitly treats her as innocent in the open country scenario, verse 26.

options.text: The man is never held responsible in either scenario.
options.note: Verse 25 explicitly states the man alone must die in the open country scenario.

options.text: The law gives no such distinction.
options.note: Verses 23-27 give a detailed, explicit distinction.

quiz.q: Whom does verse 30 forbid a man from marrying?

options.text: His father's wife, so as not to dishonor his father's marriage bed.
options.note: Verse 30 states this explicitly.

options.text: Any woman from a foreign nation.
options.note: That is not the subject of this verse; see chapters 7 and 23 for laws about foreign nations.

options.text: A widow of any kind.
options.note: The prohibition is specific to a father's wife, not widows generally.

options.text: No marriage restrictions are given here.
options.note: Verse 30 gives an explicit restriction.

questions: The law required you to actively return a neighbor's lost property rather than ignore it. Where do you find it easier to look away from an inconvenient responsibility toward someone else's loss?
Tassels were meant to be a visible, daily reminder to keep God's commands. What small, physical habit or reminder in your life actually points you back to obeying God?
The law took seriously the harm of a false accusation against a bride's honor. Where might you need to guard your own words so you do not damage someone's reputation without real evidence?

This chapter's careful distinction between assault and consent reflects real concern for the vulnerable, even within an ancient legal system. How does God's evident concern for victims of assault shape how you listen to and care for someone who has been harmed?
A woman touched the tassel of Jesus' garment in faith and was healed. Where do you need to reach out to Christ in simple faith rather than trying to fix your own situation first?

translationNotes.note: 'Wool and linen woven together' reflects a broader biblical principle against mixing distinct created kinds, seed, animals, fabric, also stated in Leviticus 19:19; the exact rationale is not explained but consistently treats mixture of these specific kinds as a matter of holiness.
'Tassels' translates tsitsit, fringes worn on the four corners of a garment; Numbers 15:38-40 explains their purpose as a visual reminder to keep all the LORD's commandments.

The distinction between the city and the open country reflects the ancient court's method of judging consent by whether a cry for help could plausibly have been heard; in both settings the law's concern is to protect a genuinely assaulted woman and hold the guilty man responsible.
